topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stood at the edge of the clearing, his hands gripping the rough bark of the remaining stump. The air felt suddenly thin, the usual afternoon warmth replaced by a prickling chill that settled deep in his chest. Where the towering oak had stood just a few years ago, a gaping, raw wound marred the landscape. He squinted, trying to reconcile the memory—the frantic digging in the soil, the hopeful watering—with the stark reality before him. His breath hitched, a small, involuntary sound escaping his throat. He took a hesitant step closer, his boots crunching on fallen debris. "No," he whispered, the sound barely audible above the rustle of unseen leaves. His gaze swept over the barren earth, searching for any sign, any misplaced branch, anything that suggested a recent presence. Nothing. Just the silence where a landmark used to be.

[story 2]

Sarah walked slowly toward the boundary line, her pace measured, almost unnaturally so. The sight of the stumps made her stomach clench, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath her ribs. She stopped dead, her shoulders slumping as she surveyed the devastation. The tree, her childhood companion, was gone. The space it occupied was now just uneven ground, stripped bare. A tremor ran through her hands; she clenched them into fists until her knuckles were white. "This can't be right," she murmured, her voice tight, strained. She circled the area twice, her eyes darting everywhere, cataloging the absence. A sudden wave of nausea washed over her, making her lean against a nearby fence post, trying to steady the quickening pulse beneath her skin.

[story 3]

Mark approached the site with a heavy tread, his jaw visibly tight. He stopped several yards from the exposed roots, his posture rigid, every muscle in his body coiled with tension. The scale of the removal was undeniable; it wasn't just a pruning, it was an excision. He lowered his gaze to the ground, his eyes unfocused for a moment as he tried to process the betrayal of the scene. A sharp intake of breath escaped him. "Gone?" he asked the empty air, his tone flat, devoid of its usual easy cadence. He shook his head once, a jerky, sharp movement, as if trying to dislodge a sudden, unwelcome thought. He took a deep, shuddering breath, the air tasting metallic in his mouth, before forcing himself to look back at the destruction.
